summary: Danish-American photojournalist and social reformer (1849–1914). Best known for How the Other Half Lives (1890), a muckraking exposé of New York City tenement conditions using flash photography. Famous photographs include Bandit's Roost and Five Cents a Spot. Theodore Roosevelt called him 'the best American I ever knew.' Pioneered indoor flash photography using magnesium powder.

works.name: How the Other Half Lives (1890)
works.description: Riis's landmark muckraking photojournalistic book, subtitled 'Studies among the Tenements of New York.' Documents slum conditions on the Lower East Side, including Mulberry Street's 'Bend.' Theodore Roosevelt read it and sent a note: 'I have read your book, and I have come to help.' Led to demolition of the Mulberry Bend slum and construction of a park. Title drawn from Rabelais's Gargantua and Pantagruel. Wikipedia

works.name: Bandit's Roost (1888)
works.description: Riis's most famous individual photograph, showing a menacing group of men standing in a back alley at 59½ Mulberry Street. Clotheslines above the alley became a symbol of the 'poverty line.' The alley is in what was then the 'Bend' of Little Italy. Wikipedia

clues.clue: At least ten threatening men staring at the viewer in a back alley at 59½ Mulberry Street
examples: In one work of art by this man, at least ten threatening looking toughs stare at the viewer while standing in a back alley at 59 ½ Mulberry Street.
This Danish-American pioneer of photojournalism captured many pictures of the New York slums, including one at 59 ½ Mulberry Street, known as Bandit's Roost.

clues.clue: Clotheslines in this photo (and others of Mulberry Street) represent the 'poverty line'
examples: Clotheslines in this artist's photos of Bandit's Roost and other Mulberry Street locations represent the 'poverty line.'

clues.clue: In How the Other Half Lives alongside In the Home of an Italian Ragpicker / The Madonna of the Slums
examples: Those photos are titled In the Home of an Italian Ragpicker and Bandit's Roost, though the former is often known as The Madonna of the Slums.

works.name: Five Cents a Spot / Lodgers in Bayard Street (1889)
works.description: Photograph of lodgers crammed into a dirty bedroom on Bayard Street, illustrating the cheapest possible urban lodging. Subtitled 'Five Cents a Spot.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Set at Bayard Street; shows lodgers crammed into a dirty bedroom; subtitled 'Five Cents a Spot'
examples: Another of his artworks, subtitled 'Five Cents a Spot,' is set at Bayard Street and shows lodgers crammed into a dirty bedroom.

comprehensive_summary:
Jacob Riis (1849–1914) was a Danish-American photojournalist and social reformer who became one of the most important early documentary photographers in American history. An immigrant from Denmark who settled in New York, Riis worked as a police reporter covering the Lower East Side's most impoverished neighborhoods, and used his access to produce a landmark body of photographic and journalistic work exposing the lives of the urban poor.

His magnum opus, How the Other Half Lives (1890), subtitled 'Studies among the Tenements of New York,' was a muckraking photojournalistic book that documented conditions in the slums of New York City with an unprecedented combination of text and photographs. Its title was drawn from a phrase in Rabelais's Gargantua and Pantagruel. Among its most powerful images are Bandit's Roost, showing a threatening crowd of men in an alley at 59½ Mulberry Street — the heart of the notorious Mulberry Bend in Little Italy — and Lodgers in Bayard Street, subtitled 'Five Cents a Spot,' showing immigrants crammed into a squalid bedroom. Clotheslines strung across Mulberry Street in his photographs became symbols of the 'poverty line.'

Riis was a technical pioneer: he was among the first photographers to use indoor flash photography, producing light by igniting magnesium powder. He once accidentally set himself on fire in the process. He photographed gangs of street children he called 'Street Arabs' and 'growler gangs,' and often titled his photographs by their cost in cents. His book had an immediate and far-reaching impact: Theodore Roosevelt, then a New York City Police Commissioner, read it and came to Riis in person, writing 'I have read your book, and I have come to help.' Roosevelt later called Riis 'the best American I ever knew,' and the two went on nighttime walks through the slums to find sleeping police officers. The book directly led to the demolition of the Mulberry Bend slum and the construction of Columbus Park, the first park in the Five Points neighborhood.
